fragments.content: Volume of distribution (Vd) is a theoretical volume relating the amount of drug in the body to its plasma concentration: Vd = Amount of drug / Plasma concentration. It indicates the extent of drug distribution beyond plasma into tissues. Vd does not represent a real physiologic volume but reflects tissue affinity. Small Vd (~3-5L, plasma volume): drugs stay in circulation (warfarin, heparin - highly protein bound). Moderate Vd (~14L, extracellular fluid): aminoglycosides. Large Vd (>40L, total body water or more): extensive tissue distribution (digoxin Vd=500L, chloroquine Vd=15,000L). High Vd drugs are poorly removed by dialysis.

Vd determines loading dose calculation: Loading dose = Target concentration × Vd / Bioavailability. For drugs with large Vd, substantial loading doses are needed to achieve therapeutic levels quickly (e.g., digoxin, amiodarone). In obesity: lipophilic drugs (benzodiazepines, propofol) distribute into adipose tissue requiring weight-based dosing using total body weight; hydrophilic drugs (aminoglycosides) use adjusted body weight. In elderly: decreased total body water reduces Vd for hydrophilic drugs, increasing plasma concentrations. In ascites/edema: increased extracellular fluid increases Vd for hydrophilic drugs, potentially requiring higher doses. Vd changes in pregnancy due to increased plasma volume and body fat.

Vd is calculated from plasma concentration measurements after IV administration: Vd = Dose / C0 (concentration at time zero, extrapolated from elimination curve). Apparent Vd at steady state (Vdss) accounts for tissue equilibration. Central volume (Vc) represents initial distribution space before tissue equilibration. Multi-compartment models describe drugs with distinct distribution and elimination phases. Factors affecting Vd: molecular size (large molecules stay in plasma), lipophilicity (lipid-soluble drugs have high Vd), protein binding (high binding reduces Vd), tissue binding (increases Vd), ionization (charged molecules have lower Vd). Pathologic states alter Vd: burns increase Vd due to capillary leak; dehydration decreases Vd.
